does: This paper identifies and addresses the generation bottleneck in synchronous RLHF training, where variable response lengths cause GPU underutilization during decoding. The authors propose PAT, an adaptive tensor parallelism method that dynamically reconfigures the degree of tensor parallelism across GPU clusters during the generation stage of each RLHF iteration. PAT combines two components: a predictor-guided online reconfiguration strategy that decides when and how to reconfigure TP based on offline profiling data, triggering changes only when latency improvements exceed reconfiguration costs, and a lightweight state update mechanism that minimizes overhead by selectively updating only the affected model states and tensor layouts rather than performing full reinitialization.
connects: This work builds on the established use of RLHF for large language model post-training and extends research on efficient distributed training through tensor parallelism. It responds to the long-standing challenge of load imbalance in data-parallel and model-parallel training, where synchronous execution causes idle time as fast workers wait for slow ones. The paper combines insights from dynamic computational graph optimization, online scheduling with profiling-based prediction, and efficient model reconfiguration techniques that have been developed across systems and ML infrastructure research, adapting these ideas specifically to the unique characteristics of RLHF's generation stage.
enables: This work opens pathways for more efficient RLHF scaling and could inspire similar adaptive parallelism strategies in other training paradigms with inherent load skew. The predictor-guided reconfiguration framework could extend to other dynamic reconfiguration problems in distributed training, such as adaptively adjusting pipeline parallelism or communication patterns. The lightweight state update mechanisms developed here may inform future work on efficient model reconfigurations for mixed-precision training, dynamic architecture changes, or heterogeneous cluster environments. More broadly, it suggests that static hardware configurations may be unnecessarily constraining in modern large-scale training, motivating further exploration of responsive infrastructure that adapts to workload characteristics in real time.
